1:
"And it came to pass, that, as the people pressed upon him to hear the word of God, he stood by the lake of Gennesaret," The witnesses dwell on the eager crowds, the setting, and the figure of the ships.

The people thronged him for love of his word and works. Chrysostom: "they clung to him with love and admiration, and longed to keep him... for who would depart while he performed such miracles?" When he spoke "they listen to him in silence, interrupting not the chain of his discourse." Gill notes they "bore hard upon him, and were ready to press him down" in their eagerness.

The setting is the lake of Gennesaret. Bede explains it is "the same as the sea of Galilee or the sea of Tiberias" — called Galilee "from the adjacent province," Tiberias "from a neighbouring city," and Gennesaret from the lake's own nature, its waters "constantly agitated by the breezes." Theophylact notes that "the Lord seeks to avoid glory the more it followed him," and so withdrew into a ship (cf. Matthew 13:2); Chrysostom, that "being on the sea, he fishes for those who were on the shore."

The fathers read the ships mystically. Bede: "the two ships represent circumcision and uncircumcision... the fishermen are the doctors of the Church, because by the net of faith they catch us, and bring us... to the land of the living." That the nets were being washed teaches "every time is not fitted for teaching." "The ship of Simon is the primitive Church," "well called one, for in the multitude of believers there was one heart and one soul." Augustine adds that "from which ship he taught the multitude, for by the authority of the Church he teaches the Gentiles." The witnesses thus see in the crowded shore and the borrowed boat both a true scene of Christ's popularity and a figure of his Church: the Teacher seated in Peter's ship, casting the net of the gospel over the listening multitude. Gregory Nazianzen finds in the whole scene Christ's condescension, stooping "in order that he might draw forth a fish from the deep, i.e. man swimming in the ever-changing scenes and bitter storms of this life"; the Teacher in the boat is already at his work of catching men.
2:
"And saw two ships standing by the lake: but the fishermen were gone out of them, and were washing their nets." The witnesses dwell on the boats, the fishermen, and the telling detail of the washing.

The "ships" were small fishing boats. Clarke insists "it is highly improper to term these ships," for "they appear to have been only such small boats as are used to manage nets on flat smooth beaches"; Ellicott notes "the Greek word being a diminutive" — "boats, or little ships" (cf. John 6:23). Barnes cites Josephus that there were many such on the lake, "attended by four or five men each," and small enough that "a single large draught of fishes endangered them." The two boats belonged, the narrative implies, to Jonas's sons (Peter and Andrew) and to Zebedee.

The detail of washing the nets the witnesses find significant. Ellicott marks "a slight, but noticeable variation here, from the mending their nets in St. Matthew and St. Mark": the washing "implied that, having fished fruitlessly during the night, they were now giving up the work, and cleaning their nets from weeds, etc., before laying them up." Robertson notes Luke's precise verb (eplynon, imperfect) "for cleaning," one of several distinct washing-words Luke uses.

The witnesses thus see the scene set with a quiet realism that heightens the miracle to come. The fishermen have toiled all night and caught nothing; their work is over, their nets being cleaned and folded away; the boats lie idle by the shore. Into this picture of fruitless labour and abandoned effort Christ is about to step, and from these empty, just-washed nets he will draw a catch that breaks them. The very ordinariness of the moment — weary men, idle boats, washed nets — prepares the reader to see, in what follows, not a chance success but the hand of the Lord turning the disciples' failure into an overflowing harvest at his word. Robertson notes that fishing on this lake was uncertain by its very nature — a dozen casts might bring up only weeds — so that the empty nets being washed were no reproach to the fishermen's skill but the common lot of their trade, against which the coming draught would shine the brighter.
3:
"And he entered into one of the ships, which was Simon's, and prayed him that he would thrust out a little from the land. And he sat down, and taught the people out of the ship." The witnesses dwell on the borrowed pulpit and the manner of Christ's teaching.

The boat was Simon Peter's. Theophylact (on v. 1) had marked "the gentleness of Christ; he asks Peter; and the willingness of Peter, who was obedient in all things." Christ "prayed" — that is, asked — him to put out a little, the crowd pressing too hard on the shore for him to be heard (Clarke). Robertson notes the nautical verb (epanagagein) for putting a ship out to sea.

"He sat down" in the posture of a teacher. Barnes notes "this was the common posture of Jewish teachers; they seldom or never spoke to the people standing" (cf. Matthew 5:1). The arrangement, Barnes suggests, was likely "a small bay or cove," where "the people on the shore stood round him in the form of an amphitheatre," and "the smooth still water of the lake" carried his voice (Clarke).

The witnesses delight in the picture. Robertson, with Plummer: "Christ uses Peter's boat as a pulpit whence to throw the net of the Gospel over his hearers." Barnes: "no spectacle could be more sublime than that of the Son of God... speaking from the bosom of a placid lake — the emblem of the peaceful influence of his own doctrines — to the poor, the ignorant, and the attentive multitudes assembled on the shore." Augustine (on v. 1) read the putting-out "a little from the land" as a lesson in moderation: that the multitude be "neither taught earthly things, nor from earthly things rush into the depths of the sacraments." The witnesses thus see Christ pressing a fisherman's boat into the service of the kingdom — and, in so doing, beginning to draw its owner into a higher fishing than he yet knew. Robertson adds that Christ "would seem to have chosen this mode of teaching not unfrequently," the boat giving him both a clear voice over the water and a refuge from the crush of the eager crowd that had pressed upon him on the shore.
4:
"Now when he had left speaking, he said unto Simon, Launch out into the deep, and let down your nets for a draught." The witnesses dwell on the command, its grace, and its mystical reach.

Having taught, Christ rewards the lender of the boat. Cyril: "having sufficiently taught the people, he returns again to his mighty works, and by the employment of fishing fishes for his disciples." Chrysostom notes Christ's condescension: "he called the wise men by a star, the fishermen by their art of fishing." Theophylact adds that the Lord "left not the master of the ship without reward, but conferred on him a double kindness, giving him first a multitude of fishes, and next making him his disciple."

The fathers read the deep mystically. Gregory Nazianzen: Christ condescends "in order that he might draw forth a fish from the deep, i.e. man swimming in... the bitter storms of this life." Ambrose notes that "to Peter alone it is said, Launch out into the deep, i.e. into deep researches. What is so deep as the knowledge of the Son of God?" The "nets" are "the interweaving of words... of the Apostles," which "kill not the fish that are caught, but keep them safe" and "bring up those that are tossing about in the waves... to the regions above."

The catch foreshadows the gospel harvest. Cyril: "this was a figure of the future; for they will not labour in vain who let down the net of evangelical doctrine, but will gather together the shoals of the Gentiles." Augustine distinguishes this miracle from the post-resurrection draught of John 21:6, where the nets did not break and the fish were counted — "this would seem to have reference to the event which Luke relates, when from the multitude of the fishes the nets were broken." The witnesses thus see in the command both a real gift to Peter and a prophecy of his apostleship: bidden to launch into the deep, the fisherman is being called to the deeper waters of the knowledge of Christ and the catching of men. Bede draws the further note that the filling of the ships "goes on until the end of the world"; the single draught at Christ's word stands for the whole long ingathering of the Church, in which the apostles and their successors let down the gospel net age after age.
5:
"And Simon answering said unto him, Master, we have toiled all the night, and have taken nothing: nevertheless at thy word I will let down the net." The witnesses dwell on the title Peter gives, his weary candour, and his obedient faith.

The title "Master" is distinctive. Ellicott notes the word (epistatēs) "is not the same as that (didaskalos, teacher) in the other Gospels... and is peculiar to St. Luke"; it "implies a less distinct recognition of our Lord's character as a teacher or Rabbi, and was more the language of general respect, such as workmen might use of their master." Robertson: it is "used only by Luke... always in addresses to Christ," and "recognizes Christ's authority." Yet, Ellicott adds, Peter's words imply "the previous discipleship which we learn from John 21:3" and the first chapter of John.

Peter's reply is honest weariness. "We have toiled all the night, and have taken nothing" — Robertson notes the verb (kopiaō) carries "the notion of weariness in toil," and Peter "calls attention also to the whole night of fruitless toil." Fishing this lake "was Peter's business, and he really claimed superior knowledge on this occasion to that of Jesus."

Yet he obeys. "Nevertheless at thy word I will let down the net" — "a remarkable instance of faith," says Barnes: "though it seemed so improbable... he was willing to trust the word of Jesus." Calvin notes Peter "highly esteemed Christ, and had the highest respect for his authority," so that "when worn out by fruitless toil, he commences anew what he had already attempted in vain." The witnesses thus see in Peter's answer the model of obedient faith: against his own experience and his trade's wisdom, on the bare word of Christ he lets down the net — and Barnes draws the lesson, "if all would as readily obey him, all would be in like manner blessed" (cf. Psalm 127:1). Clarke presses the ministerial lesson: "he who assumes the character of a fisher of men, under any authority that does not proceed from Christ, is sure to catch nothing; but he who labours by the order and under the direction of the great Shepherd and Bishop of souls cannot labour in vain."
6:
"And when they had this done, they inclosed a great multitude of fishes: and their net brake." The witnesses dwell on the great catch and the precise sense of the breaking net.

The draught was enormous. Robertson notes the effective aorist "they shut together" (synekleisan) "a great multitude of fishes" — where the whole night's toil had yielded nothing, a single cast at Christ's word fills the net to bursting.

The "net brake" the commentators carefully qualify. Barnes: "or their net began to break, or was about to break. This is all that is implied in the Greek word. If their nets had actually broken... the fish would have escaped." Clarke cites the grammatical rule that "verbs which signify the accomplishment of a thing are often to be understood as only signifying the beginning of that accomplishment" — so "began to break... was likely to be broken." Ellicott and Robertson agree: the imperfect (dierēsseto) means "the nets were actually tearing in two," straining under the weight, "and so they would lose all the fish" unless helped. Ellicott notes this is precisely why Luke does not, as is sometimes thought, contradict John's later miracle where the net held.

The witnesses read the miracle's purpose with Calvin: "the design of the miracle undoubtedly was to make known Christ's divinity, and thus to induce Peter and others to become his disciples." From it he draws "a general instruction, that we have no reason to be afraid lest our labour should not be attended by the blessing of God and desirable success, when it is undertaken by the authority and guidance of Christ" (cf. Ecclesiastes 11:6). The witnesses thus see the breaking, straining net as the visible token of Christ's power over the creatures and the deep: the same word that had taught the multitude now commands the fish, and the fisherman who trusted that word beyond his own skill is rewarded with a catch that proclaims his Master's divinity. Gill notes the fish were enclosed only after they had "put the ship out further to sea, and let down their net" in the deep, exactly as Christ had directed — so that the abundance answered point for point to the obedience, and the blessing followed the precise doing of his word.
7:
"And they beckoned unto their partners, which were in the other ship, that they should come and help them. And they came, and filled both the ships, so that they began to sink." The witnesses dwell on the call for help, the partners, and the overwhelming abundance.

The catch outran one crew. They "beckoned" — gave signs — Robertson suggests "possibly they were too far away for a call to be understood," or, as Cyril (on v. 4) says, Peter was "unable to speak from astonishment at the draught." The "partners" Robertson distinguishes: metochoi here, those in business "company under Simon's lead," and koinōnoi in v. 10 (James and John), with "the notion of personal fellowship" (cf. Philippians 4:3). Both boats "filled" until "they began to sink" — Robertson notes the inchoative present, "beginning to sink," not actually sunk.

The fathers read the second ship mystically. Augustine (on v. 4): "the other ship is Judea, out of which James and John are chosen... that they might fill both ships," for "at the name of Jesus every knee shall bow, whether Jew or Greek"; Bede, that it is "the Church of the Gentiles," filled when "the Lord finds not in Judea so many believers as he knows are destined to eternal life."

Clarke draws a practical lament from the cooperation. "Had not these been called in to assist, the net must have been broken, and all the fish lost. What a pity there should be such envious separation among the different sects that profess to believe in Christ! Did they help each other in the spirit of Christian fellowship, more souls would be brought to the knowledge of the truth." The witnesses thus see in the two over-laden, sinking boats a parable of gospel success: the harvest is too great for any one company of labourers, and only by calling in their partners — by the joining of hands across the Church — can the catch be brought safely in (cf. Galatians 6:2). Barnes observes that "every kind of fishing is uncertain" — sometimes "every net is full" and sometimes empty "without any other apparent reason than that of throwing it on the right side of the ship" — which only magnifies the hand of Christ in turning a barren night into a catch that two boats could scarcely hold.
8:
"When Simon Peter saw it, he fell down at Jesus' knees, saying, Depart from me; for I am a sinful man, O Lord." The witnesses dwell on Peter's awe, his confession of sin, and the reverence beneath his words.

The miracle pierced Peter's conscience. Bede: "Peter was astonished at the divine gift, and the more he feared, the less did he now presume." Cyril explains the fear: "calling back to his consciousness the crimes he had committed, he is alarmed and trembles, and as being unclean, he believes it impossible he can receive him who is clean; for he had learnt from the law to distinguish between what is defiled and holy." Barnes: "in amazement, wonder, and gratitude, and not doubting that he was in the presence of some divine being, he prostrated himself to the earth" (cf. Isaiah 6:5).

"Depart from me" is the cry of humility, not of dislike. Barnes notes "it was an expression of Peter's humility, and of his consciousness of his unworthiness." Ambrose turns it to counsel: "say you also, Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, O Lord, that God may answer, Fear not. Confess your sin, and the Lord will pardon you."

The fathers also read the scene mystically. Augustine hears Peter speak "in the character of the Church full of carnal men" — who "with the utterance of their deeds... persuade" the good "to go away, that they may not be governed by the good," even while "all the more anxiously" they "pay honours to them," as Peter fell at Christ's feet yet said "depart." Yet, Bede notes, "the Lord allays the fears of carnal men, that no one trembling at the consciousness of his guilt... might be afraid to undertake the journey of holiness." The witnesses thus see in Peter's fall the right response of a sinner to the manifest presence of God: not presumption but prostration, not a casual welcome but a trembling confession — and they mark that Christ, far from departing, answers such humility with "Fear not," drawing nearer to the very man who owned himself unworthy.
9:
"For he was astonished, and all that were with him, at the draught of the fishes which they had taken;" The witnesses dwell on the amazement that gripped the whole crew and its cause.

The wonder was overwhelming and shared. Robertson renders it vividly: "literally, for a wonder held him round" — "it held Peter fast and all the rest." Ellicott: "more literally, for astonishment seized him." Gill notes "all that were with him" — Andrew his brother, and "the servants they had with them to manage the vessel" — were caught up in the same amazement.

The cause was the sheer impossibility of the catch. After a whole night's fruitless toil by skilled fishermen, a single cast at Christ's word had filled two boats to sinking. The men knew their trade and knew the lake; they understood at once that what they had seen lay beyond all natural explanation.

Gregory of Nyssa (on v. 8) draws out the deeper ground of the wonder: "when Christ commanded to let down the nets, the multitude of the fishes taken was just as great as the Lord of the sea and land willed. For the voice of the Word is the voice of power, at whose bidding at the beginning of the world light and the other creatures came forth" (cf. Psalm 33:9, "he spake, and it was done"). The same Word that created the fish now summoned them into the net. The witnesses thus see the astonishment as the fitting recognition of divine power: these were not credulous men easily impressed, but seasoned fishermen confronted with a draught no skill could account for. Their wonder, like Peter's fall, is the soul's instinctive response to the presence of the Creator, and it prepares them — astonished, humbled, and attentive — to hear the great word of calling that Christ is about to speak. Augustine (on v. 8) notes that Andrew, though not named here, is understood from Matthew and Mark to have been in the ship; the whole company of fishermen, partners and servants alike, stood together in one amazement, so that the witnesses to the miracle were many and the testimony to Christ's power beyond dispute.
10:
"And so was also James, and John, the sons of Zebedee, which were partners with Simon. And Jesus said unto Simon, Fear not; from henceforth thou shalt catch men." The witnesses dwell on the partners, the calming word, and the great commission to catch men alive.

James and John shared the wonder and the work. Ellicott notes the word here for "partners" (koinōnoi) differs from that in v. 7, expressing "a more general partnership in business" (cf. Philemon 1:17) — a personal fellowship, not mere shared labour.

To Peter's fear Christ speaks peace and promise. "Fear not," Barnes notes, "he calmed their fears... and to their surprise announced that henceforward they should be appointed as heralds of salvation." The promise is "thou shalt catch men" — Luke's equivalent (Ellicott) of "I will make you fishers of men" in Matthew and Mark (cf. Matthew 4:19). The verb (zōgreō) is striking. Clarke: "thou shalt catch men alive; this is the proper signification." Robertson presses it: "Peter is to be a catcher of men, not of fish, and to catch them alive and for life, not dead and for death" (cf. 2 Timothy 2:26) — "the great Pentecost will one day prove that Christ's prophecy will come true."

Calvin sees here a call to office, not merely to faith. The four "were not only gathered by Christ to be his disciples, but were made apostles... a special call to a particular office." He marks Christ's choice of "rough mechanics — persons not only destitute of learning, but inferior in capacity — that he might train, or rather renew them by the power of his Spirit, so as to excel all the wise men of the world," humbling "the pride of the flesh." The witnesses thus see the catch interpreted: the miracle was a parable of the men's future ministry. As the net had gathered the fish at Christ's word, so these fishermen, renewed by his Spirit, would gather multitudes of men out of the troubled sea of the world — and gather them alive, unto life.
11:
"And when they had brought their ships to land, they forsook all, and followed him." The witnesses dwell on the completeness of the renunciation and the harmony of the Gospels.

What they left was little, but it was their all. Barnes: "it was not much that they left — a couple of small boats and their nets; but it was all they had, even all their living. But this showed their love of Jesus, and their willingness to deny themselves, as really as if they had forsaken palaces and gold" (cf. Philippians 3:7). The lesson, he adds, is that "all that Jesus asks is that we should leave all we have for him... and be willing to give them up when he requires it." Robertson notes "they left their business for active service of Christ," and bids "other business men to pause and see if Jesus is calling them to do likewise."

The obedience was prompt and whole. Chrysostom (on v. 8) marks "their faith and obedience": "though they were eagerly engaged in the employment of fishing, yet when they heard the command of Jesus, they delayed not, but forsook all and followed him."

The witnesses harmonize this with the earlier meetings. Wesley notes "they had followed him before (John 1:43), but not so as to forsake all; till now, they wrought at their ordinary calling." Ellicott and Augustine (on v. 8) allow that there may have been "a temporary return... to their former calling" between the call by the Jordan and this decisive forsaking (as again in John 21). Augustine even reads the bringing of the ships to land and forsaking all as a figure of "the end of time, when those who have clung to Christ shall altogether depart from the storms of this world." The witnesses thus see in this verse the decisive break: men who had known and believed in Christ now leave their nets for good, exchanging the catching of fish for the catching of men — a pattern, the commentators urge, of the wholehearted following that Christ asks of all who hear his call.
12:
"And it came to pass, when he was in a certain city, behold a man full of leprosy: who seeing Jesus fell on his face, and besought him, saying, Lord, if thou wilt, thou canst make me clean." The witnesses dwell on the leper's worship, his humble faith, and the confession in his words.

The man was "full of leprosy" — the disease at its worst. Ambrose notes "no definite place is mentioned where the leprous man was healed, to signify that not one people of any particular city, but all nations were healed." Athanasius marks his worship: "the leper worshipped the Lord God in his bodily form, and thought not the Word of God to be a creature because of his flesh... in a created temple he adored the Creator of all things, falling down on his face."

His posture and words breathe humility and faith. Ambrose: "in falling upon his face he marked his humility and modesty... his reverence kept not back his confession; he shows his wound, and asks for a remedy." Of "if thou wilt" Ambrose says he "doubted not from distrust of his mercy, but checked by the consciousness of his own unworthiness" — "a confession full of devotion and faith, placing all power in the will of the Lord." Cyril notes "he knew that leprosy yields not to the skill of physicians," but had seen Christ's divine cures; Titus of Bostra draws the lesson "to commit the whole to the will of God, who knows what is best for us."

The words "if thou wilt, thou canst" the fathers find rich in doctrine. Cyril: "from majesty alone proceeds the royal command; how then is the Only-begotten counted among the servants, who by his mere will can do all things?... whatsoever things are of the same power are wont to be of the same substance." The witnesses thus see in this full leper a model suppliant: prostrate in worship, owning his uncleanness, yet so sure of Christ's power that he leaves only the will to him — the very pattern of a faith that submits its desire to the wisdom and mercy of God (cf. Matthew 8:2; Leviticus 13:45).
13:
"And he put forth his hand, and touched him, saying, I will: be thou clean. And immediately the leprosy departed from him." The witnesses dwell on the forbidden touch, the sovereign word, and the instant cure.

Christ heals as he was asked. Ambrose: "he heals in the same manner in which he had been entreated" — answering "if thou wilt" with "I will." Gill notes he touched the man "having compassion on him, and commiserating his sad case." The touch itself is marvellous. "The law forbids to touch the leprous man," Ambrose observes, "but he who is the Lord of the law submits not to the law, but makes the law"; he touched "not because without touching he was unable to make him clean, but to show that he was neither subject to the law, nor feared the contagion." Theophylact adds that "his sacred flesh has a healing and life-giving power, as being indeed the flesh of the Word of God" — so that the touch which by law defiled the clean here cleansed the unclean.

The word reveals the two natures. Ambrose: "he says, I will, for Photinus" (who denied his deity); "he commands, for Arius"; "he touches, for Manichaeus" (who denied his flesh). Gregory of Nyssa: "the soul wills, the body touches" — "in each are evident the signs of a heavenly nature."

The cure was instant and total. "Immediately the leprosy departed from him" — as when Naaman's flesh "came again like unto the flesh of a little child" at the prophet's word (cf. 2 Kings 5:14). Ambrose marks "there is nothing intervening between God's work and his command, that we may see in the inclination of the healer the power of the work." The witnesses thus see in the touch and word of Christ the union of compassion and majesty: he stoops to touch the untouchable, fearless of defilement because he cannot be defiled, and by a single word of will drives out in a moment a disease no physician could cure — at once true man who touches and true God who commands. Cyril marks the wonder of one who heals "both divinely and bodily" — "for it is of God so to will that all things are done accordingly, but of man to stretch forth the hand"; from the two natures is perfected one Christ, for the Word was made flesh.
14:
"And he charged him to tell no man: but go, and shew thyself to the priest, and offer for thy cleansing, according as Moses commanded, for a testimony unto them." The witnesses dwell on the charge of silence, the duty to the priest, and the testimony.

The command to silence the fathers read variously. Ambrose (on v. 12): Christ "taught us that our good deeds are not to be made public, but rather... concealed, that we should abstain not only from gaining money, but even favour"; or "the cause of his commanding silence was that he thought those to be preferred who had rather believed of their own accord than from the hope of benefit." Yet, Cyril adds, "though the leper was silent, the voice of the transaction itself was sufficient to publish it."

The man is sent to the priest. Robertson notes the direction "to go to the priest to receive a certificate showing his cleansing, like our release from quarantine" (cf. Leviticus 14:2–32). Christ thus upholds the Mosaic law: the cleansed leper must submit to the appointed inspection and offer the appointed gift. Chrysostom (on v. 12) adds that, since "men, when they are sick, remember God, but when they recover wax dull," Christ "bids him always keep God before his eyes, giving glory to God."

"For a testimony unto them" the witnesses take of the priests and people. Robertson notes the phrase, with the same wording in Mark and Matthew (cf. Matthew 8:4), refers loosely "to people in general." The cleansed leper, presenting himself and his offering, would be living proof both that he was truly healed and that One had come who could cleanse leprosy — a witness laid before the very priesthood that judged such cases. The witnesses thus see Christ acting with perfect propriety: he heals by his own power, yet sends the man through the lawful channel, that the cure might be officially attested, the law honoured, and the priests themselves confronted with evidence of the Healer's power, whether they would receive it or no. Clarke notes that all nations have offered such gratitude-gifts for benefits received from the object of their worship; here the cleansed man's offering, made as Moses commanded, both fulfilled the law and gave God the glory due for so great a deliverance.
15:
"But so much the more went there a fame abroad of him: and great multitudes came together to hear, and to be healed by him of their infirmities." The witnesses dwell on the spreading report and the gathering crowds.

The charge of silence had the opposite effect. Gill notes "the more he charged the man to keep silence, the more he blazed it abroad, being elated with the cure he received, and filled with gratitude to his benefactor." Robertson observes that Mark "tells more about the effect of this disobedience"; the fame of Jesus "kept going" — the imperfect tense marking continuous spread.

The crowds came in answer. "Great multitudes came together," Robertson notes, again the imperfect: "the more the report spread, the more the crowds came" (cf. Matthew 4:25). Ellicott marks that "both the verbs, went and came together, are in the tense that implies continuous action" — a steady, swelling movement of people toward Jesus.

Their purpose was twofold: "to hear, and to be healed... of their infirmities." The witnesses note that the multitudes sought both his word and his cures. The healing of the leper, which Christ had wished to keep quiet, became instead a magnet drawing the whole region to him. There is in this a certain irony the commentators feel: the man's grateful disobedience, though against Christ's command, spread the knowledge of his mercy far and wide, so that crowds who needed healing — of body and of soul — pressed to hear and be made whole. Yet the very success, as the next verse shows, drove Christ not deeper into the crowds but away from them, into the desert to pray. The witnesses thus see here the gathering tide of his Galilean popularity, and prepare the reader for the contrast: that the Saviour, sought by multitudes, sought in turn the solitude of communion with his Father. Barnes notes the multitudes came from far and wide (cf. Matt 4:25), drawn by the report of his cures; the healing of one outcast leper had, against Christ's own wish for quiet, made his name ring through the whole country and gathered the sick and the curious in one great throng.
16:
"And he withdrew himself into the wilderness, and prayed." The witnesses dwell on Christ's habitual retreat and his life of prayer.

The withdrawal was frequent and deliberate. Clarke renders it "he frequently withdrew into the desert," holding "this to be the import of the original words"; Wesley agrees "the expression in the original implies that he did so frequently." Robertson notes the periphrastic imperfect — "he himself was withdrawing in the desert places and praying" — and that the verb (hypochōreō), "perhaps with the notion of slipping away secretly," occurs in the New Testament only in Luke. Ellicott marks the plural, "into the wildernesses... now in one part, now in another, of the unenclosed, uncultivated country."

The motive was prayer. Gill notes he withdrew "that he might have rest from the fatigues of preaching and healing... and being alone, and free from company, might have an opportunity for private prayer." The detail "and prayed," Ellicott observes, "is peculiar to St. Luke, who throughout his Gospel lays stress on this feature in our Lord's life." Robertson draws the picture: "the more the crowds came... the more Jesus turned away from them to the desert regions and prayed with the Father," for "the wild enthusiasm of the crowds was running ahead of their comprehension of Christ."

The witnesses press the example upon ministers. Clarke: the Lord teaches "that they are to receive fresh supplies of light and power from God by prayer, that they may be the more successful in their work... A man can give nothing unless he first receive it." He laments, "Why is there so much preaching, and so little good done? Is it not because the preachers... keep too long in the crowd, and are so seldom in private with God?" The witnesses thus see in Christ's retreat the secret of his power and the rebuke of all merely busy religion: at the height of his popularity, sought by multitudes, the Saviour slips away to be alone with his Father — showing that the strength to serve the crowds is drawn, not in their midst, but in the wilderness of prayer (cf. Mark 1:35; Matthew 14:23).
17:
"And it came to pass on a certain day, as he was teaching, that there were Pharisees and doctors of the law sitting by... and the power of the Lord was present to heal them." The witnesses dwell on the gathered critics and the healing power present in Christ.

The Pharisees and doctors had come from everywhere to watch. Cyril notes "the Scribes and Pharisees who had become spectators of Christ's miracles heard him also teaching." The "power of the Lord present to heal" he takes of Christ's own inherent power: "not as though he borrowed the power of another, but as God and the Lord he healed by his own inherent power... the divine power went on abounding in giving remedies."

The occasion called for a proof. Cyril: "because it was necessary, where so great a number of Scribes and Pharisees had come together, that something should be done to attest his power before those men who slighted him, he performed the miracle on the man with the palsy" — one "who, since medical art seemed to fail, was carried by his kinsfolk to a higher and heavenly Physician."

Bede draws out the order of the cure to come: "the Lord, about to cure the man of his palsy, first loosens the chains of his sins, that he may show him that on account of the bonds of his sins he is punished with the loosening of his joints, and that unless the former are set free, he cannot be healed." The witnesses thus see the scene set for a decisive encounter: the learned opponents assembled in force, the helpless paralytic brought by faith, and Christ — teaching with authority and bearing in himself the very power of God to heal — about to reveal, before his critics' eyes, that he can loose not only palsied limbs but the deeper bonds of sin (cf. Mark 2:1; Matthew 9:2). Cyril adds that, though men often receive spiritual gifts and then fall away from the giver's rule, "it was not so with Christ, for the divine power went on abounding"; the fountain of healing in him was inexhaustible, ready for every need that faith might bring.
18:
"And, behold, men brought in a bed a man which was taken with a palsy: and they sought means to bring him in, and to lay him before him." The witnesses dwell on the bearers, the sufferer's condition, and their determined effort.

The man was carried by friends. Gill notes "four men brought him, as Mark says," and "they sought means to bring him in" to the house where Jesus was (cf. Mark 2:3). The sufferer himself was wholly helpless, dependent on the love and labour of those who bore him.

Luke describes the ailment with a physician's precision. Ellicott notes "taken with a palsy" is "literally paralysed, or palsy-stricken, a somewhat more technical, and therefore characteristic word than the sick of the palsy in the other Gospels." Robertson agrees that Luke's phrase (paralelumenos) "is the technical medical term (Hippocrates, Galen)" rather than "Mark's vernacular word." The detail accords with the tradition that Luke was a physician, attentive to the exact nature of disease (cf. Matthew 9:2).

Their effort was earnest and persistent. Robertson notes "they sought" is a "conative imperfect" — they kept trying to find a way in. The crowd around Jesus was so great that the ordinary approach was blocked, yet the bearers would not be turned back. The witnesses thus see in this opening of the scene a beautiful picture of intercessory faith: men who could do nothing for their friend's disease themselves, but who would spare no labour to bring him to the One who could. Their persistence — refusing to give up when the door was shut — is the first mark of the faith that Christ will presently commend, and a pattern of how the helpless are to be carried, by the determined love of others, into the presence of the Saviour. Robertson notes that Luke's careful diagnostic language throughout his Gospel — naming the precise disease rather than the common term — accords with the tradition of "the beloved physician," whose interest in the exact nature of the man's affliction makes the cure that follows the more striking.
19:
"And when they could not find by what way they might bring him in because of the multitude, they went upon the housetop, and let him down through the tiling with his couch into the midst before Jesus." The witnesses dwell on the bold expedient and the faith it displayed.

Barred at the door, they found another way. Chrysostom (on v. 17) admires them: "they are to be admired who brought in the paralytic, since on finding that they could not enter in at the door, they attempted a new and untried way." Robertson explains the means: "the flat roof of Jewish houses was usually reached by outside stairway" (cf. Acts 10:9), and they let him down "through the tiles" — Mark "speaks of digging a hole in this tile roof" (cf. Mark 2:4). Wesley pictures it: "going up the stairs on the outside, they came upon the flat-roofed house, and let him down through the trap door... with such circumspection as the circumstances plainly required."

The "couch" Luke names with a diminutive (klinidion), Ellicott notes, "to show how it was that the process described was possible" — a small pallet light enough to lower. Robertson pictures "the four friends... probably each holding a rope to a corner of the pallet" — "a moment of triumph over difficulties and surprise to all in the house."

The witnesses find the whole scene a triumph of faith over obstacles. These men would not let the crowd, the closed door, or the labour of climbing and unroofing stand between their friend and Christ. They brought him, against every difficulty, "into the midst before Jesus" — the very place he needed to be. The commentators see in this a lesson for all who would bring others to the Saviour: that real faith is inventive and undeterred, finding a way where none seems open, counting no trouble too great to lay a needy soul at the feet of the Lord. It was this faith, shown in their ingenious persistence, that Christ was about to honour. Gill notes they could find no way in "by the door, or in at a window of the house," so great was the press; only the roof remained, and to it their love resorted, that nothing might keep their friend from the Healer.
20:
"And when he saw their faith, he said unto him, Man, thy sins are forgiven thee." The witnesses dwell on the faith Christ saw, the word of pardon, and the link of sin and suffering.

The faith was shared. Robertson notes "their faith" is "in all three Gospels" — the faith of the four bearers, and of the sufferer too. Chrysostom (on v. 17): "there was combined in this the faith also of the sufferer himself, for he would not have submitted to be let down, had he not believed." Christ rewards the faith of the friends in the man, and the man's own with them.

Ambrose marvels at intercession honoured: "mighty is the Lord, who pardons one man for the good deed of another... If you despair of the pardon of heavy sins, bring the prayers of others, bring the Church to pray for you, and at sight of this the Lord may pardon what man denies."

The word of pardon comes first, before the cure. Bede (on v. 17) had explained the order: the palsy was "punishment" for "the bonds of his sins," and "unless the former are set free, he cannot be healed" (cf. John 5:14). Augustine notes that "Man, thy sins are forgiven" conveys both that the man, "in that he was man, could not say, I have not sinned," and "that he who forgave sins might be known to be God." Robertson observes Luke's "Man" where Mark and Matthew have "Son" or "child." The witnesses thus see Christ going at once to the root: the paralytic was brought for his body, but Christ first heals his soul, granting the deeper deliverance no friend could obtain. He saw their faith and answered it — but answered it first with the gift the man most needed, the forgiveness of his sins, before ever he touched the palsy. Chrysostom (on v. 17) presses the lesson of order: "if we suffer bodily, we are enough concerned to get rid of the hurtful thing; but when there has harm happened to the soul, we delay" — whereas Christ here heals the soul first, teaching us where the true and deeper danger lies.
21:
"And the scribes and the Pharisees began to reason, saying, Who is this which speaketh blasphemies? Who can forgive sins, but God alone?" The witnesses dwell on the critics' charge and the truth hidden in their error.

The scribes reasoned in secret. Chrysostom (on v. 17) notes that "from fear of the multitude, the Pharisees durst not openly expose their designs, but only meditated them in their hearts." Their charge was blasphemy, for which "it was commanded in the law that whoever blasphemed God should be punished with death" (Ambrose).

Their premise was true. "They say true," Bede observes, "that no one can forgive sins but God" (cf. Isaiah 43:25; Psalm 103:3). The error lay not in the principle but in its application: "who yet forgives through those to whom he gives the power of forgiving. And therefore Christ is proved to be truly God, for he is able to forgive sins as God." Ambrose marks the madness: "great is the madness of an unbelieving people, who, though they have confessed that it is of God alone to forgive sins, believe not God when he forgives sins."

Clarke draws the dilemma sharply: "if Jesus were not God, he could not forgive sins; and his arrogating this authority would have been blasphemy." So Christ, "that these scribes and Pharisees might have the fullest proof of his Godhead, works in their presence three miracles" — "the remission of the poor man's sins, the discernment of the secret thoughts of the scribes, and the restoration of the paralytic." The witnesses thus see the scribes, by their own true confession, condemned out of their own mouths: they rightly held that God alone forgives, and stood in the presence of One forgiving sins. Their reasoning, meant to convict Christ of blasphemy, in fact pointed straight to his deity — for the only escape from the charge was the very truth they would not see, that this man was God. Ambrose adds that "from his very works the Son of God receives testimony," for "it is more powerful evidence when men confess unwillingly"; the scribes' own admission, wrung from them against their will, became an unwitting witness to the deity of the One they opposed.
22:
"But when Jesus perceived their thoughts, he answering said unto them, What reason ye in your hearts?" The witnesses dwell on Christ's reading of the heart and what it proved.

Jesus knew their unspoken reasonings. Gill notes he perceived their thoughts "being God omniscient." Ellicott refines the word: "their reasonings, the Greek noun being formed from the verb used in" the previous verse — the very inward debate the scribes had thought secret. Robertson notes the participle (epignous) is "common verb for knowing fully," the same form Mark uses.

This reading of the heart was itself a sign. Clarke (on v. 21) counted "the discernment of the secret thoughts of the scribes" the second of the three miracles by which Christ proved his Godhead — for to know the hidden counsels of the heart belongs to God alone, who "searcheth the hearts" and needs not that any should testify of man (cf. John 2:25). The scribes had reasoned "within themselves," confident none could hear; yet Christ answered their thoughts as plainly as if they had spoken aloud.

So the very objection became its own refutation. They had asked, "Who can forgive sins but God alone?" — and the One they charged with blasphemy now showed himself to possess the divine knowledge of the heart, which is as much God's prerogative as the forgiving of sins. Chrysostom (on v. 17) urges the deeper lesson: "let us then remove the fountain of evil, and the waters of sickness will cease to flow." The witnesses thus see Christ meeting his critics' silent malice with a quiet demonstration of deity: before he heals the body or vindicates the word of pardon, he lays bare their hearts — proving, to any who would see, that the One who forgives sins also reads the secret reasonings of men, and so is indeed the God they confessed (cf. Mark 2:8). Ellicott notes the noun for their "thoughts" is built from the very verb of their reasoning in the previous verse, so that Christ answers not a vague mood but the exact argument they had silently framed — proof that nothing in the heart is hidden from him.
23:
"Whether is easier, to say, Thy sins be forgiven thee; or to say, Rise up and walk?" The witnesses dwell on the searching question Christ puts to his critics.

Gill explains the force of the question. Christ had said to the palsied man, "Thy sins are forgiven," which had given offence to the scribes "imagining that he had assumed too much to himself"; "wherefore he proposes the following case to them, which they thought was most easy for man, or more proper and peculiar to God to say." The question turns their objection back upon them.

Both sayings, the witnesses observe, lie beyond mere human power; both are properly the work of God alone. But there is a difference in the proof. To say "thy sins are forgiven" is, in one sense, the easier word, because no eye can see whether it is done; a man might claim it without being disproved. To say "rise up and walk" is harder, for it submits itself at once to the test — if the man does not rise, the speaker is exposed. Christ therefore offers to do the visible, verifiable thing, that his power to do the invisible may be believed.

The witnesses thus see in the question a piece of unanswerable reasoning. The scribes held that to forgive sins belonged to God alone; Christ proposes to perform, before their eyes, a healing that equally belongs to God alone, and to do it on the spot — so that the cure they can see shall vouch for the pardon they cannot. The harder, open act will prove the authority behind the easier, hidden one. By framing the matter so, Christ leaves his critics no escape: if he can raise the paralytic with a word, then his word of forgiveness is no empty blasphemy but the act of One who has indeed the power of God. The visible miracle is made the seal of the invisible grace, that they "may know" what manner of person stands before them. Clarke had numbered (on v. 21) three miracles wrought here to prove Christ's Godhead — the pardon, the reading of thoughts, and the cure — and this question binds the last to the first, offering the visible healing as the warrant of the invisible forgiveness, that no honest mind could refuse the inference.
24:
"But that ye may know that the Son of man hath power upon earth to forgive sins, (he said unto the sick of the palsy,) I say unto thee, Arise, and take up thy couch, and go into thine house." The witnesses dwell on the declared purpose, the title Son of man, and the parenthesis.

The cure is given for a stated reason: "that ye may know." Robertson notes the purpose clause (hina eidēte) is the same in all three Gospels. Christ heals the body precisely to prove his authority over sin. The title "Son of man" the witnesses take as asserting both natures. Gill: the scribes "took him for a mere man, in which they were mistaken; for though he was really a man, and the Son of man, yet he was God as well as man — God manifest in the flesh." The "power upon earth to forgive sins" is thus claimed by One who is more than he seemed.

The parenthesis the commentators find striking. Robertson notes "this same parenthesis right in the midst of the words of Jesus is in Mark and Matthew" — "(he said unto him that was palsied)" — and calls it "conclusive proof of interrelation between these documents," the words of Jesus "quoted practically alike in all three Gospels" (cf. Mark 2:10).

The command itself is threefold: arise, take up the couch, go home. Christ bids the man not only stand but carry the bed that had carried him, and walk away — a complete and public restoration (cf. John 5:8). The witnesses thus see Christ answering his own question of the previous verse. Having asked which is easier to say, he now does the harder, visible thing in proof of the easier, hidden one: he speaks, and the paralytic will rise. The healing is no mere act of mercy but an argument — the seal set by heaven upon his claim to forgive, so that the very men who charged him with blasphemy must reckon with a deed only God could do, wrought to vindicate a word only God could speak.
25:
"And immediately he rose up before them, and took up that whereon he lay, and departed to his own house, glorifying God." The witnesses dwell on the instant cure and the healed man's praise.

The cure was immediate and complete. Gill notes that "as soon as ever these words were spoken by Christ, the man, before sick of the palsy, finding himself perfectly well, got off of his couch, and stood up on his feet before the Scribes and Pharisees, and all the people." Robertson notes Luke's phrase "that whereon he lay" — the imperfect, "upon which he had been lying down" — in place of repeating the word for couch. The bed that had borne the helpless man he now bore himself.

The proof was public and undeniable. He rose "before them" — in the very sight of the assembled critics who had questioned Christ's authority. There was no concealment, no delay; the man who had been carried in through the roof walked out through the crowd on his own feet, the palsy gone in an instant at a word.

His response was praise. "Glorifying God" — and Ellicott notes "the fact that the man himself did this, as well as the by-standers, is peculiar to St. Luke." Robertson adds, "as one can well imagine." The witnesses thus see the argument of the preceding verses brought to its proof: Christ had said the man would rise that they might know his power to forgive, and the man rose. The visible miracle has vindicated the invisible pardon. And the cured man, restored in both soul and body, does not boast in himself but gives the glory to God — the right and instinctive response of one who has received from Christ a double deliverance, going home not only healed but forgiven, with praise on his lips for the God whose power he had felt (cf. Mark 2:12). Ellicott marks that the man's own act of glorifying God, joined to that of the bystanders, is a detail peculiar to Luke; the one most changed by the miracle was the first and readiest to give God the praise, his restored body becoming at once an instrument of thanksgiving.
26:
"And they were all amazed, and they glorified God, and were filled with fear, saying, We have seen strange things to day." The witnesses dwell on the crowd's amazement, fear, and confession.

The amazement was overwhelming. Robertson notes the word (ekstasis) means "something out of its place, as the mind" — "the people were almost beside themselves." Gill marks that it was "the common people," not "the Pharisees and doctors of the law," who "glorified God"; the critics remained unmoved while the multitude was carried away.

With the wonder came fear. They "glorified God" and at the same time "were filled with fear" — Robertson notes the imperfect "kept glorifying God" alongside the aorist "were filled with fear." The sense of God's presence and power in their midst stirred both adoration and awe, as the manifest working of the divine always does (cf. Acts 4:21).

Their confession the witnesses find characteristic of Luke. "We have seen strange things to day" — the word (paradoxa), Clarke explains, is "our very word paradox... something that appears false and absurd, but is not really so; something contrary to the commonly received opinion." Robertson and Ellicott note the precise expression — "things beyond expectation" — is "peculiar to St. Luke." Wesley sums what they had seen: "sins forgiven, miracles wrought." The witnesses thus close the scene on the crowd's response, which stands in pointed contrast to the scribes': where the learned reasoned and resented, the people wondered, feared, and glorified God. They could not explain what they had witnessed, but they knew it was the work of God — strange, unlooked-for, beyond all their expectation — and that the power of heaven had passed among them that day in the person of the One who both forgave the sinner and raised the paralytic (cf. Mark 2:12). Clarke explains a paradox as "something that appears false and absurd, but is not really so" — and the crowd, faced with a deed they would have thought impossible were the evidence not before their eyes, could only confess that they had seen what lay beyond all their expectation.
27:
"And after these things he went forth, and saw a publican, named Levi, sitting at the receipt of custom: and he said unto him, Follow me." The witnesses dwell on the publican called, the grace of the call, and his instant obedience.

Levi is Matthew. Augustine: "this is Matthew, also called Levi." Bede notes that "Luke and Mark, for the honour of the Evangelist, are silent as to his common name, but Matthew is the first to accuse himself, and gives the name of Matthew and publican, that no one might despair of salvation because of the enormity of his sins, when he himself was changed from a publican to an Apostle" (cf. 1 Timothy 1:13).

His former life made the call the more wonderful. Cyril: "Levi had been a publican, a rapacious man, of unbridled desires after vain things, a lover of other men's goods... but snatched from the very worship of malice by Christ's call." The "Follow me," he adds, bids him follow "not with bodily step, but with the soul's affections."

The obedience was immediate. Chrysostom marks "both the power of the caller, and the obedience of him that was called. For he neither resisted nor wavered, but forthwith obeyed; and like the fishermen, he did not even wish to go into his own house." Basil notes the cost: "he not only gave up the profits of the customs, but also despised the dangers which might occur... from leaving the accounts of the receipts uncompleted." Theophylact draws the image: "from him that received toll... Christ received toll, not money, but entire devotion." The witnesses thus see in Levi's call the triumph of grace over the most despised of callings: at a single word the grasping tax-gatherer rises from his lucrative seat and follows, leaving gain and security behind — a standing proof that none, however sunk in sin, is beyond the reach of Christ's summons (cf. Matthew 9:9). Chrysostom marks too the wisdom of the call's timing: Christ "displayed every sort of remedy", correcting some by discoursing, some by curing, some by rebuking, and some, as here, by the very summons to follow — so that no class of sinner was beyond the variety of his saving art.
28:
"And he left all, rose up, and followed him." The witnesses dwell on the completeness of the renunciation and the constancy of the following.

The forsaking was total. Robertson notes "this detail" — "he forsook all" — "in Luke alone": "he left his profitable business for the service of Christ." Gill lists what went: "his company, his business, and all the profits of it." Clarke presses it: "completely abandoning his office, and every thing connected with it. He who wishes to preach the Gospel, like the disciples of Christ, must have no earthly entanglement... The concerns of his own soul, and those of the multitudes to whom he preaches, are sufficient to engross all his attention."

The following was continual. Ellicott notes "followed him — not then only, but continually, the verb being in the imperfect tense"; Robertson agrees it is "imperfect active, perhaps inchoative" — "he began at once to follow him and he kept it up."

Calvin guards against a false inference. The forsaking does not mean Matthew "abandoned the charge of his own domestic affairs"; as Paul's image of the soldier (cf. 2 Timothy 2:4) does not mean soldiers "divorce their wives, forsake their children, and entirely desert their homes," but that they "quit their homes for a time... that they may be wholly employed in war" — so "nothing kept Matthew from following where Christ called; and yet he freely used both his house and his property, as far as the nature of his calling allowed." The witnesses thus see in Levi's rising the model of wholehearted discipleship: he gave up his gainful trade without hesitation, counting all loss for Christ (cf. Philippians 3:8), and entered upon a following that was not a single act but the settled course of his life. Wesley sums the renunciation in three words — "leaving all, his business and gain" — and the witnesses note how the brevity of the record matches the decisiveness of the act: there is no recorded hesitation, no bargaining, only a man who rose and went. Robertson observes that where Luke writes the imperfect "followed", Mark and Matthew have the aorist, perhaps ingressive — the one stressing that Levi kept following ever after, the others that he began to follow then and there; both together give the full picture of a discipleship at once begun and lifelong.
29:
"And Levi made him a great feast in his own house: and there was a great company of publicans and of others that sat down with them." The witnesses dwell on the feast, its purpose, and the company.

The feast was lavish and grateful. Ellicott notes the word (dochē) "means literally a reception"; Robertson, "Levi made Jesus a big reception." Gill suggests Levi, "a rich man, in gratitude to Christ... made this entertainment," and "had also another view in it, to bring him into the company of his fellow publicans, hoping he might be useful to them, as he had been to him; for of this nature is true grace, to wish for... the salvation of the souls of others."

Matthew, tellingly, omits it from his own Gospel. Barnes draws the lesson: "this fact shows how little inclined the evangelists are to say anything in favour of themselves... True religion does not seek to commend itself... it leaves its good deeds to be spoken of, if spoken of at all, by others" (cf. Proverbs 27:2).

The company was a motley one. Robertson: "none but social outcasts would eat with publicans at such a feast... It was a motley crew that Levi had brought together, but he showed courage as well as loyalty to Jesus." Ellicott notes it is "characteristic of St. Luke as a Gentile that he will not use the word sinners" here but "the vaguer word others." The witnesses thus see in Levi's feast the first-fruits of his conversion: scarcely called, he gathers his old associates to meet his new Master, turning his house into a mission. And Christ, who came to seek the lost, does not refuse the table of publicans but reclines among them — at the risk of being called "a friend of publicans and sinners" (cf. Matthew 11:19) — that he might do them good. Barnes wishes "all the great feasts that are made were made in honour of our Lord," with him a welcome guest. Clarke adds that the "great feast" denotes chiefly "the number of the guests, and the manner in which they were received", a gathering of Levi's friends and acquaintance assembled "that they might be convinced of the propriety of the change he had made" when they heard his heavenly Teacher.
30:
"But their scribes and Pharisees murmured against his disciples, saying, Why do ye eat and drink with publicans and sinners?" The witnesses dwell on the murmurers, their charge, and its true source.

The critics were uninvited onlookers. Robertson notes the word "murmured" (egoggyzon) is "a picturesque onomatopoetic word that sounds like its meaning... like the buzzing of bees." They "were not invited to this feast and would not have come if they had been. But, not being invited, they hang on the outside and criticize." The phrase "their scribes" Robertson defends from Mark's "the scribes of the Pharisees" (cf. Mark 2:16); "it is only the Pharisees who find fault here."

The charge was aimed through the disciples at the Master. Augustine (on v. 27) notes Luke reports it as objected "to the disciples also, that the charge might be understood both of him and them": "seeing the disciples ate with publicans and sinners, it was the rather objected to their Master, as him whom they followed and imitated" (cf. Luke 15:2). Ellicott notes that in reporting others' words, Luke "naturally gives the word sinners as it was actually spoken."

Ambrose hears in the murmur an older voice. "This was the voice of the Devil. This was the first word the Serpent uttered to Eve, Yea, hath God said, Ye shall not eat. So they diffuse the poison of their father." The witnesses thus see the Pharisees' complaint for what it was: not honest inquiry but envious fault-finding, carping from the sidelines at a fellowship they would not share and could not understand. They counted it a scandal that Christ should eat with sinners; but the scandal, the witnesses observe, lay in their own cold hearts, which grudged the lost the very mercy that might have saved them — and which, in murmuring against grace, echoed the serpent's first insinuation against the goodness of God. Robertson pictures the scene: the crowd so large that "the feast may have been served out in the open court at Levi's house, a sort of reclining garden party", which let the uninvited critics stand at the edge and find fault with what they had no part in.
31:
"And Jesus answering said unto them, They that are whole need not a physician; but they that are sick." The witnesses dwell on the proverb and the image of Christ the Physician.

Christ takes up the cause of his disciples. Gill notes that, "knowing that they aimed at him, though, according to this evangelist, they only mentioned his disciples, he takes up the cause, and vindicates both himself and them" by the proverb of the physician.

Luke's wording bears the physician's stamp. Ellicott: "better, they that are in health. Note, as once more characteristic of the physician, the use of this term instead of they that are strong, the strict meaning of the Greek word used in the other two Gospels." Robertson agrees that "whole" (hygiainontes) "is the usual word for good health used by Greek medical writers," where Mark and Matthew have "those who have strength" (cf. Matthew 9:12).

The image both answers and indicts. Chrysostom (on v. 27): Christ "reminds them of their common infirmities, and shows them that they are of the number of the sick, but adds, He is the Physician." A physician, he notes, "unless he touch the afflicted part cannot cure the disease" — so Christ must go among sinners to heal them. The witnesses thus see Christ turning the Pharisees' complaint into a confession of their own need. They thought themselves "whole" and despised the company of the "sick"; but the very fact that they felt no need of the Physician was the surest sign of their disease. Christ's eating with publicans was not, as they supposed, a contamination, but the necessary work of the soul's Physician, who must come where the sick are to make them well — as the prophet asked, "Is there no balm in Gilead; is there no physician there?" (cf. Jeremiah 8:22). The proverb leaves the murmurers self-condemned: by scorning the sick, they only proved that they would not be healed. Robertson notes that this same medical vocabulary recurs through Luke's Gospel and the Acts, the mark of "the beloved physician" who naturally reached for the language of health and sickness in recording the Great Physician's own words.
32:
"I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance." The witnesses dwell on the purpose of Christ's coming and the meaning of "the righteous."

The words declare Christ's whole errand. Gill: "I came not to call the righteous — such as the Scribes and Pharisees were in their own apprehension... who trusted in themselves that they were righteous"; "but sinners to repentance — such as the publicans," whom he "not only called to repentance by the outward ministry of the word, but brought them to it" (cf. 1 Timothy 1:15).

"To repentance" is Luke's careful addition. Ellicott and Robertson note "the last word is added by St. Luke only," not genuine in Mark or Matthew. Its force is vital. Chrysostom (on v. 27): "so far am I from hating sinners, that for their sakes only I came — not that they should remain sinners, but be converted and become righteous." Augustine adds it "serves well to explain the passage, that no one should suppose that sinners, because they are sinners, are loved by Christ"; the sick are called "in order that from iniquity, as from sickness, they should be saved."

"The righteous" the witnesses take of the self-satisfied. Ambrose (on v. 27): Christ "meant by the righteous those who boast of the law, and seek not the grace of the Gospel; for no one is justified by the law, but redeemed by grace." Robertson notes that "for the moment Jesus accepts the Pharisaic division between righteous and sinners to score them"; elsewhere he shows "they only pretend to be righteous." The witnesses thus see Christ stating the very principle his conduct embodied: he came as a Physician for the sick, a Saviour for sinners, not to flatter those who fancied themselves whole. His call is to repentance — to a real turning from sin to righteousness — and it finds its welcome not among the proud who think they need nothing, but among those who, owning their sin, will be healed (cf. Luke 15:7). Robertson adds that Jesus here "blazed the path for all soul-winners", who learn that "the self-satisfied are the hard ones to win and they often resent efforts to win them to Christ", while those who own their sickness gladly receive the Physician.
33:
"And they said unto him, Why do the disciples of John fast often, and make prayers, and likewise the disciples of the Pharisees; but thine eat and drink?" The witnesses dwell on the new charge and the kinds of fasting.

The critics press from one objection to another. Cyril: "as soon as they have received the first answer from Christ, they proceed from one thing to another, with the intent to show that the holy disciples, and Jesus himself with them, cared very little for the law." First they had charged eating with sinners; now, that his disciples did not fast as John's and the Pharisees' did (cf. Matthew 9:14).

Augustine distinguishes two fasts: "one is in tribulation, to propitiate God for our sins; another in joy, when, as carnal things delight us less, we feed the more on things spiritual." The Lord, "being asked why his disciples did not fast, answered as to each fast." The fast of mortification has its place; but its purpose, Cyril notes, is "that by the mortification of their body they may quell its passions" — a need that did not press on Christ's joyful company in the same way, for "being made partakers of his grace without fasting, they were strengthened in all holy and godly living."

The fathers also harmonize the accounts. Augustine notes Luke reports the question as spoken "by others concerning them," where Matthew has John's disciples ask "why do we and the Pharisees fast" — "unless that they themselves also came, and were all eager... to put the question." The witnesses thus see the Pharisees and John's stricter disciples joining to fault the freedom of Christ's followers. The objection assumed that true piety must wear the face of austerity; but Christ will answer that there is a time for joy as well as for mourning, and that to impose the fast of sorrow upon a wedding-feast is to mistake the very nature of the hour his coming had brought. Cyril notes the deeper point that Christ "needed not fasting for the perfecting of virtue, since as God he was free from every yoke of passion"; when he did fast forty days, it was "not to mortify his passions, but to manifest to carnal men the rule of abstinence" — an example, not a personal necessity.
34:
"And he said unto them, Can ye make the children of the bridechamber fast, while the bridegroom is with them?" The witnesses dwell on the figure of the bridegroom and the joy of his presence.

Christ answers with the image of a wedding. Chrysostom: "as if he should say, The present time is one of joy and gladness; sorrow must not then be mixed up with it." Wesley puts the question's force: "is it proper to make men fast and mourn during a festival solemnity?" The form is emphatic — Ellicott notes it "is somewhat stronger... than the simple Can the children of the bride-chamber fast?" — and, Robertson adds, with the negative it "expects the answer no."

The fathers unfold the figure. Cyril: "the showing forth of our Saviour in this world was nothing else but a great festival, spiritually uniting our nature to him as his bride, that she who was formerly barren might become fruitful" (cf. John 3:29). The "children of the bridechamber" are "those who have been called by him through a new and evangelical discipline — but not the Scribes and Pharisees, who observe only the shadow of the law."

Ambrose presses the application to believers: "we cannot fast who have Christ, and banquet on the flesh and blood of Christ"; Basil, that the children of the Bridegroom "cannot fast, i.e. refuse nourishment to the soul, but live on every word which proceeds out of the mouth of God." The witnesses thus see Christ vindicating the gladness of his disciples as wholly fitting. While the Bridegroom was among them, their hearts were a wedding-feast; to have made them mourn and fast would have been as out of place as sorrow at a marriage. Their not fasting was no laxity but the proper joy of those who had found the long-awaited Christ — a joy that the austere onlookers, knowing only the shadow of the law, could neither share nor understand (cf. Matthew 9:15). Augustine adds that this very saying, found only in Luke — "ye cannot make the children of the bridegroom fast" — points back at the questioners themselves, who would soon make those children mourn by taking the Bridegroom from them in death.
35:
"But the days will come, when the bridegroom shall be taken away from them, and then shall they fast in those days." The witnesses dwell on the coming loss and the fasting it would bring.

Christ foretells a change. Gill notes "the days will come — and that in a very little time, as they did." The "bridegroom taken away" the fathers read of the passion. Augustine (on v. 33): the very men who asked the question "would make the children of the Bridegroom mourn and fast, since they were about to kill the Bridegroom"; then "shall they be desolate, and in sorrow and lamentation, until the joy of consolation shall be restored to them by the Holy Spirit" (cf. John 16:20; Isaiah 22:12).

The fast that then comes is the fast of true mortification. Ambrose (on v. 33): "that fast is not given up whereby the flesh is mortified, and the desires of the body chastened, for this fast commends us to God." Bede: "as long as the Bridegroom is with us we both rejoice, and can neither fast nor mourn; but when he has gone away through our sins, then a fast must be declared and mourning be enjoined."

Ambrose adds a searching caution. Christ said, "I will be with you always, even to the end of the world"; how then can the Bridegroom be taken away? "No one can take Christ away from you," he answers, "unless you take yourself away from him." The witnesses thus see Christ both defending his disciples' present joy and providing for the future. Fasting was not abolished but reserved for its proper season: when the Bridegroom should be crucified and withdrawn, sorrow and fasting would have their fitting place. And in the deeper sense Ambrose draws, the Bridegroom is still with his Church; the seasons of spiritual barrenness and mourning come not from his absence but from our turning away — and the remedy is to return to him in whose presence is fullness of joy. Barnes notes the fast then to come would be no mere ceremony but the genuine sorrow of bereaved hearts (cf. Isa 22:12), the natural mourning of those whose Lord had been crucified — fasting in its truest and most fitting season, not the affected austerity the Pharisees demanded.
36:
"And he spake also a parable unto them; No man putteth a piece of a new garment upon an old; if otherwise, then both the new maketh a rent, and the piece that was taken out of the new agreeth not with the old." The witnesses dwell on the parable and the folly it exposes.

Christ adds a third illustration. Robertson notes "there are three parables here... the bridegroom, the patch on the garment, the wineskin," though "they are not called parables save here" by Luke. Wesley: the parable, "taken from clothes and wine," was "peculiarly proper at a feast."

The better text heightens the folly. Ellicott notes the best manuscripts read "No man having rent a piece from a new garment putteth it upon an old," so that "what folly could be greater than the act described?" — for "he will both rend the new, and the patch from the new will not agree with the old." Robertson notes Luke's word "rendeth" (schisas, whence our "schism") is his alone here, where the result in Mark and Matthew is "a worse rent."

The fathers read the garments spiritually. Ambrose (on v. 33): Christ "calls fasting an old garment, which the Apostle thought should be taken off, saying, Put off the old man with his deeds" (cf. Colossians 3:9); "in the same manner we have a series of precepts not to mix up the actions of the old and new man." The witnesses thus see Christ teaching that his new gospel is not a patch to be sewn onto the worn garment of Pharisaic legalism. To force the old austerities upon his joyful disciples would ruin both — tearing the new and misfitting the old. The gospel is a whole new garment, not a mending of the old; and to mingle the rigid forms of the law with the freedom of grace is to spoil the one without saving the other (cf. Matthew 9:16). Augustine offers a further reading: once "the gift of the Holy Spirit being received, there is a kind of fast which is of joy", proper to those "already renewed to a spiritual life"; before that gift they are as old garments, to which a new patch is most unsuitably joined.
37:
"And no man putteth new wine into old bottles; else the new wine will burst the bottles, and be spilled, and the bottles shall perish." The witnesses dwell on the wineskins and the incompatibility they picture.

The "bottles" are skins. Clarke notes "the eastern bottles are made of skins, generally those of goats." New wine, still fermenting, must not be poured into old, brittle skins: "these old bottles would not be able to stand the fermentation of the new wine, as the old sewing would be apt to give way." The result is loss on every side — Ellicott: "the new wine will burst the bottles, and be spilled, and the bottles shall perish."

Gill reads the old skins as the unyielding Pharisees. The Scribes and Pharisees "are here compared" to old bottles, "into whose hearts the new wine of Gospel grace was not put" — to whom "the love of God, comparable to new wine," and "the blessings of the new covenant of grace," and "the truths of the Gospel," could not be entrusted, their hard and settled prejudice unable to contain them.

The witnesses thus see the parable carrying forward the lesson of the patch. The new wine is the living power of the gospel — its joy, its grace, its truth, still working and expanding. The old skins are hearts hardened in legal religion, rigid and unable to stretch. To pour the one into the other would be to lose both: the gospel's life would be spilled, and the old forms would burst. Christ's point against the fault-finders is plain: his disciples cannot be confined within the stiff observances of Pharisaic piety, for the new life he has given them would only rend such bonds. A new spirit requires new forms; the wine of grace must have vessels supple enough to hold it (cf. Job 32:19). Ellicott notes the verbs match — "the new wine will burst the bottles, and be spilled, and the bottles will perish" — a threefold ruin underscoring that the mismatch destroys not the old vessel only but the precious wine as well, so that nothing is gained and all is lost by the forced combination.
38:
"But new wine must be put into new bottles; and both are preserved." The witnesses dwell on the fitting vessels and the joy they hold.

The new wine requires new skins. Robertson notes the verbal form "must be put" (blēteon) "appears here alone in the N.T., though it is common enough in Attic Greek" — "a survival of the literary style," expressing necessity. The lesson is that the gospel must have vessels suited to it.

The new bottles Gill reads as renewed hearts. They are "such as the disciples of Christ were, and sinners called to repentance are, who are renewed by the Spirit and grace of God." Into such vessels the new wine is safely poured, "and these are filled with spiritual joy and comfort, as with new wine, arising from discoveries of the love of God, a view of interest in the blessings of the covenant, and an application of Gospel truths and promises."

"And both are preserved." The witnesses mark the happy outcome that crowns the parable. Where new wine in old skins destroys both, new wine in new skins keeps both safe: the wine is held, and the vessel endures. So the freedom and joy of the gospel, poured into hearts made new by grace, neither bursts nor is lost, but is treasured and grows. The witnesses thus see Christ completing his answer to the fault-finders. Their austere demand would have ruined his disciples, as new wine ruins old skins; but the new wine of grace and the renewed hearts that receive it belong together and are mutually preserved. The whole series of figures — bridegroom, garment, wine — drives home one truth: a new and joyful order has come with Christ, and it cannot be cramped into the worn-out vessels of the old religion, but calls for hearts made new, in which the joy of the gospel may be kept and may flourish. Robertson notes the impersonal verbal form governs "new wine" as its object though no agent is named, the literary construction lending a certain weight to the maxim; the necessity is absolute — there simply is no safe way to keep new wine but in new skins, no way to hold the gospel's joy but in a renewed heart.
39:
"No man also having drunk old wine straightway desireth new: for he saith, The old is better." The witnesses dwell on this closing word, peculiar to Luke, and its tolerant insight.

The addition is Luke's alone. Ellicott notes "this addition is peculiar to St. Luke, and calls accordingly for distinct notice." Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic background: "old wine" is "of three years old... because from the time that the vine had produced that wine, it had put forth its leaves three times." The "old is better" — the better manuscripts, Ellicott notes, read simply "the old is good," the word (chrēstoteros) "partly implying the sense of mild"; Clarke, "more agreeable to the taste."

The imagery the witnesses interpret of habit and prejudice. Ellicott: "the old wine is the principle — in spiritual things, the religion — that animated the man's former life," here "the motive-power of the Law in its rigid and Pharisaic form"; "the new wine... is the freer, nobler, life-power of the gospel." It was "not to be wondered at that men accustomed to the older system should be unwilling to embrace the new"; the words are spoken "in a tone of something like a tolerant pity for the prejudices of age and custom."

Barnes draws the application to the disciples. The Pharisees "wish to draw my disciples to the austere and rigid duties of the ceremonial law"; but "they have tasted the gentle and tender blessings of the gospel; they have no relish for your stern and harsh requirements." To force the old austerities on them now "would be ill-timed, inappropriate, and incongruous"; "at the proper time all the sterner duties of religion will be properly regarded." The witnesses thus see Christ closing his defence with a gentle realism: men long used to the old ways naturally cling to them and find the new strange. He neither flatters the prejudice nor crushes it, but, with patient wisdom, gives the new wine time and fitting vessels — knowing that those who have truly tasted the gospel will not, in the end, go back to the sour vintage of mere legal religion (cf. Jeremiah 6:16).
